summary: Gruber reviews the Verizon iPhone 4 after a week of use, concluding it's essentially the same phone on a better network. While AT&T delivers faster raw data speeds, Verizon offers more consistent coverage, noticeably superior voice quality, and fewer dead spots. The exclusive Wi-Fi hotspot tethering feature is well-implemented and potentially game-changing for iPad users. Gruber speculates the AT&T exclusivity deal simply ran through end of 2010, and frames the Verizon iPhone as a controlled experiment that benefits both Apple (proving dropped calls were AT&T's fault) and Verizon (proving their network is better with the same hardware).
key_points: Verizon's network provides better voice quality and more consistent coverage, while AT&T is faster on raw data throughput when signal is strong
The Wi-Fi hotspot feature is so well-designed it could eliminate the need for a 3G iPad — one $20 tethering fee covers all portable devices
The CDMA simultaneous voice/data limitation is a non-issue in practice, far less annoying than the original EDGE iPhone's limitations
The Verizon iPhone serves as a controlled experiment benefiting both Apple and Verizon against AT&T — same phone, better network
The delay was likely just AT&T's exclusivity contract running through 2010, not any negotiation drama over branding or control
money_quotes: It's the same phone. The only difference is the network. And Verizon's network is better.
The numbers don't lie. AT&T's data network is faster — when you have a strong connection on both phones. The catch is with that 'when you have a strong connection' clause.
If you don't like this sort of 'extend the battery life at all costs' behavior, you probably don't like the iPhone anyway.
Apple is delighted because it's a way of 'proving' that the iPhone's notorious problems with call-dropping and voice quality are AT&T's fault, not theirs.
Verizon isn't competing with Apple. They're competing against AT&T.
The iPhone, though, is a phone they don't need to own, push, or control. Apple will sell it for them. Verizon just needs to sell their core competency: cellular networking.
A lot of people have been waiting for four years for this phone. The funny thing is, by next month, the Verizon iPhone is going to seem like the most normal thing in the world.
key_insight: The Verizon iPhone is a controlled experiment that proves the iPhone's call quality problems were AT&T's fault, benefiting both Apple's reputation and Verizon's competitive position.
